elements.sent-2411-001: Let $\sM_g$ be the moduli space of hyperbolic surfaces of genus $g$ endowed with the Weil-Petersson metric.
elements.sent-2411-002: We view the regularized determinant $\log \det(\Delta_{X )$ of Laplacian as a function on $\sM_g$ and show that there exists a universal constant $E>0$ such that as $g\to \infty$, \ben \item the expected value of $\left|\frac{\log \det(\Delta_{X ) {4\pi(g-1) -E \right|$ over $\sM_g$ has rate of decay $g^{-\delta}$ for some uniform constant $\delta \in (0,1)$; \item the expected value of $\left|\frac{\log \det(\Delta_{X ) {4\pi(g-1)}\right|^\beta$ over $\sM_g$ approaches to $E^\beta$ whenever $\beta \in [1,2)$.. \een.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: Let $X$ be a closed hyperbolic surface of genus $g$ $(g>1)$.
elements.sent-2411-004: The spectrum of the Laplacian $\Delta_X$ of $X$ on $L^2(X)$ is a discrete closed subset in $\R^{\geq 0}$ and consists of eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-005: We enumerate them, counted with multiplicity, in the following increasing order: \begin{equation}0=\lambda_0(X)<\lambda_1(X)\leq \lambda_2(X) \leq \cdots \to +\infty.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-006: For $z\in \mathbb{C}$, the spectral zeta function of $X$ is given as \begin{equation}\zeta_X(z)=\sum_{i=1}^\infty \frac{1}{\lambda_i^z(X)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-007: From Weyl's Law, the function $\zeta_X(z)$ is well-defined and holomorphic when $\Re{z >1$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: The regularized determinant is usually defined by \begin{equation}\log \det (\Delta_X)\overset{def}{=}-\zeta_X'(0),\end{equation} provided that $\zeta_X(\cdot)$ has an analytic extension to $z=0$.
elements.sent-2411-009: It is known from \eg Hoker-Phong \cite{HP86 and Sarnak \cite{sarnak1987determinants that \begin{equation}\label{eq-det-zetaprime} \det (\Delta_X)=Z_0^\prime(1)e^{E\cdot 4\pi(g-1)},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}E=\frac{-1+2\log 2\pi+8\xi^\prime(-1)}{8\pi}\approx 0.0538,\end{equation} and $Z_0(s)$ is the Selberg zeta function \begin{equation}Z_0(s)=\Pi_\gamma\Pi_{k=0}^\infty \left(1-e^{(k+s)\ell_\gamma(X)}\right)\end{equation} where $\gamma$ runs over all primitive closed geodesics on $X$ and $\ell_\gamma(X)$ is the length of $\gamma$ on $X$.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: It is known by Wolpert \cite{Wol87 that the magnitudes $\left| \log \det(\Delta_X) \right|$ and $\left| \log Z_0'(1) \right|$ are proper functions on moduli space $\sM_g$ of Riemann surfaces of genus $g$, that is, they will be divergent when the surface $X$ goes to the boundary of $\sM_g$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this work, we view $\log \det (\Delta_X)$ and $\log Z_0'(1)$ as random variables with respect to the probability measure $\Prob$ on $\sM_g$ given by the Weil-Petersson metric.
elements.sent-2411-012: Naud is the first one to study their asymptotic behaviors for large genus and shows in \cite{naud2023determinants that \begin{thm* [Naud] For any $\eps>0$, \be \lim\limits_{g\to \infty}\Prob\left(X\in \sM_g; \ \frac{\left| \log Z_0^\prime(1) \right| {4\pi(g-1) <\eps\right)=1.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In the proof, Naud essentially only requires uniform spectral gaps and suitable countings of closed geodesics for large genus.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this paper, we focus on the Weil-Petersson model and use the techniques developed in \cite{nie2023large,wu2022random to show that.}
elements.sent-2411-015: There exists a uniform constant $0<\delta<1$ such that as $g\to\infty$, \begin{equation}\frac{1}{\Vol_{WP}(\sM_g)}\int_{\sM_g}\frac{\left| \log Z_0^\prime(1) \right|}{4\pi(g-1)} dX=O\left(\frac{1}{g^{\delta}}\right),\end{equation} where the implied constant is universal and $\Vol_{\mathrm{WP (\sM_g)$ is the Weil-Petersson volume of $\sM_g$.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: It would be interesting to study the optimal choice of $\delta$ in Theorem \ref{mainthm2 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: By using Markov's inequality, Theorem \ref{mainthm2 clearly implies the result of Naud above.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Moreover, combining with \eqref{eq-det-zetaprime it also implies that \begin{equation}\lim\limits_{g\to \infty}\frac{1}{\Vol_{WP}(\sM_g)}\int_{\sM_g}\frac{\left| \log \det (\Delta_X) \right|}{4\pi(g-1)} dX=E.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Indeed, our second result is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-022: For any $\beta\in (0,1)$, this is due to Naud \cite{naud2023determinants .}
elements.sent-2411-023: We say two positive functions \begin{equation}f_1(g)\prec f_2(g)\quad or \quad f_2(g)\succ f_1(g) \quad or \quad f_1(g)=O(f_2(g))\end{equation} if there exists a universal constant $C>0$, independent of $g$, such that $f_1(g) \leq C \cdot f_2(g)$; and we say \begin{equation}f_1(g) \asymp f_2(g)\end{equation} if $f_1(g)\prec f_2(g)$ and $f_2(g)\prec f_1(g)$.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}f_1(g)\prec f_2(g)\quad or \quad f_2(g)\succ f_1(g) \quad or \quad f_1(g)=O(f_2(g))\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-024: In Section \ref{s-pre we will provide a review of relevant background materials and show that for $\beta>0$, $\E\left[ \left( \frac{1 {\sys(X) \right)^\beta\right]\asymp 1$ if and only if $\beta\in (0,2)$ which will be applied in the proof of Theorem \ref{mainthm1 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Then by assuming \eqref{e-Naud-1 and closely following \cite{naud2023determinants , in Section \ref{s-mt1 we will complete the proof of Theorem \ref{mainthm1 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In the last Section, we will use the techniques developed in \cite{nie2023large,wu2022random to finish the proof of Theorem \ref{mainthm2 .}}

elements.sent-2411-030: In this section, we set up notations and basic propositions on the regularized determinant of the Laplacian and the Weil-Petersson model of random surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-031: Let $X_{g,n}$ with $2g-2+n\geq 1$ be a complete hyperbolic surface of genus $g$ with $n$ geodesic boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-032: By the Gauss-Bonnet formula, its hyperbolic area satisfies $\mathrm{Area (X_{g,n )=2\pi(2g-2+n)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: For a closed geodesic $\gamma$ on $X_{g,n}$, we use $\ell(\gamma)$ or $\ell_\gamma(X_{g,n )$ to denote its length.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Now let $X=X_g$ be a closed hyperbolic surface of genus $g$ $(g>1)$ and $\Delta_X$ be its Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-035: Recall that as in the introduction, the regularized determinant $\log \det (\Delta_X)=-\zeta_X'(0)$.
elements.sent-2411-036: Indeed, it is known from \eg \cite{HP86, sarnak1987determinants, naud2023determinants that it also satisfies the following identity \begin{equation}\label{eq-def-logdetlaplace} \log \det\Delta_X=4\pi(g-1)E+\gamma_0-\int_{0}^1\frac{S_X(t)}{t}dt-\int_1^\infty\frac{S_X(t)-1}{t}dt,\end{equation} where $\gamma_0$ is the Euler constant, $E\approx 0.0538$ is the constant given in \eqref{eq-det-zetaprime , and \begin{equation}S_X(t)=\frac{e^{-t/4}}{(4\pi t)^\frac{1}{2}}\sum_{k\geq 1}\sum_{\gamma\in P} \frac{\ell(\gamma)}{2\sinh(k\ell(\gamma)/2)}e^{-(k\ell(\gamma))^2/4t}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Here $\mathcal{P}$ is the set of oriented primitive closed geodesics on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-038: One may see \cite{naud2023determinants and references therein for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Selberg's trace formula gives the following identity: \begin{equation}\label{sel-trace-formula} \sum_{j=0}^\infty e^{-t\lambda_j}=S_X(t)+\frac{4\pi(g-1)e^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{4\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}re^{-tr^2}\tanh(\pi r)dr.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-040: See \cite[Theorem 9.5.3]{buser2010geometry or \cite[Theorem 5.6]{bergeron2016spectrum for a general form of Selberg's trace formula.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: We will apply the following two countings on closed geodesics.
elements.sent-2411-043: The first one is for general closed geodesics.
elements.sent-2411-045: Let $S$ be a hyperbolic surface of genus $g\geq 2$ and let $L>0$.
elements.sent-2411-046: There are at most $(g-1)e^{L+6}$ oriented closed geodesics on $S$ of length $\leq L$ which are not iterates of closed geodesics of length $\leq 2\arcsinh 1$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Recall that a closed geodesic $\gamma$ is called filling in a hyperbolic surface $Y$ with geodesic boundaries if each component of $Y\setminus \gamma$ is homotopic to a single point or some boundary component of $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-048: The second counting is the following bound for filling closed geodesics that is essential in the proof of Theorem \ref{mainthm2 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: One may see \cite[Theorem 4]{wu2022random or \cite[Theorem 18]{WX22pgt for more details.}}
elements.subsection-2411-005: The Weil-Petersson model of random surfaces
elements.sent-2411-050: For $2g-2+n\geq 1$, let $\T_{g,n}$ be the Teichm\"uller space of Riemann surfaces with $g$ genus and $n$ punctures.
elements.sent-2411-051: The mapping class group $\Mod_{g,n}$ acts on $\T_{g,n}$ keeping the Weil-Petersson symplectic form $\omega_{\mathrm{WP }$ invariant.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The quotient space $\M_{g,n =\T_{g,n /\Mod_{g,n}$ is the moduli space of Riemann surfaces.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: For $L=(L_1,\cdots,L_n)\in \R^n_{\geq 0}$, let $\T_{g,n (L)$ be the Teichm\"uller space of bordered hyperbolic surfaces with $n$ geodesic boundaries of length $L_1,\cdots,L_n$, and $\M_{g,n (L)=\T_{g,n (L)/\Mod_{g,n}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The Fenchel-Nielsen coordinates corresponding to a pants decomposition $\{\alpha_i\}_{i=1 ^{3g-3+n}$ of $X_{g,n}$ on the Teichm\"uller space $\T_{g,n (L)$ are of the form $X\mapsto (\ell_{\alpha_i (X),\tau_{\alpha_i (X))_{i=1 ^{3g-3+n}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Here $\tau_{\alpha_i (X)$ is the measured twist length along $\alpha_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Wolpert \cite{Wolpert82 shows that $\omega_{\mathrm{WP }$ under the Fenchel-Nielsen coordinates is given by the following theorem:.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The Weil-Petersson volume form is given by \begin{equation}d\Vol\nolimits_{WP}=\tfrac{1}{(3g-3+n)!}\underbrace{\omega_{WP}\wedge\cdots\wedge\omega_{WP}}_{3g-3+n\ copies}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-060: This is a measure on $\T_{g,n (L)$ and invariant under $\Mod_{g,n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Then it is also a measure on $\M_{g,n (L)$, which is still denoted by $d\Vol_{\mathrm{WP }$ or $dX$ for short.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Denote $V_{g,n (L)$ to be the total volume of $\M_{g,n (L)$ under the Weil-Petersson metric.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Now we consider the closed case $\sM_g$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The Weil-Petersson metric induces the Weil-Petersson probability measure $\Prob$ on $\M_g$ by: \begin{equation}\Prob(A):=\frac{1}{V_{g}}\int_{\M_{g}} 1_{A} dX\end{equation} where $\mathcal{A}\sbs\M_g$ is a Borel subset and $\mathbf{1 _{\mathcal{A }$ is its characteristic function.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The expectation of a random variable $f$ is given by \begin{equation}\E\left[ f\right]:=\frac{1}{V_g}\int_{\M_g}fdX.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-067: The following integration formula is given by Mirzakhani in \cite{mirzakhani2013growth which is very useful in the study of Weil-Petersson random hyperbolic surfaces.}
elements.sent-2411-068: In this paper we study the asymptotic behaviors of the regularized determinants of Laplacians on Weil-Petersson random surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-069: Indeed, recently the study of spectral theory of Weil-Petersson random surfaces is quite active.
elements.sent-2411-070: One may see \cite{mirzakhani2013growth, MS20, GMST21, Monk22, wu2022random, LW21, AM23, Hide21, Rud22, RW22, WX22pgt, SW22, HT22, naud2023determinants, Gong23, MS23 and references therein for recent developments on related topics.}
elements.sent-2411-071: In this subsection we recall several well-known bounds on Weil-Petersson volumes which will be applied later.
elements.sent-2411-072: It is known by \cite[Lemma 22]{nie2023large or \cite[Proposition 3.1]{MP19 that \be \left(1- c(n) \frac{t^2 {g}\right)\cdot \left(\frac{\sinh(t/2) {t/2 \right)^n\leq \frac{V_{g,n (t,\cdots,t) {V_{g,n }\leq \left(\frac{\sinh(t/2) {t/2 \right)^n\leq e^{\frac{nt {2 , \ene where $c(n)>0$ is a constant only depending on $n$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: It is also known by \cite[Part (3) of Theorem 3.5]{mirzakhani2013growth that \begin{equation}\frac{V_{g-1,2}}{V_g}=1+O\left(\frac{1}{g}\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-074: And by \cite[Lemma 3.3]{mirzakhani2013growth we know that \begin{equation}\frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{\left[\frac{g}{2}\right]}V_{i,1}\cdot V_{g-i,1}}{V_g}\asymp \frac{1}{g}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-075: These two equations above give that \be \frac{V_{g-1,2 +\sum\limits_{i=1 ^{\left[\frac{g {2}\right] V_{i,1}\cdot V_{g-i,1 {V_g}\asymp 1.. \ene.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Similar to the proof of \cite[Corollary 4.3]{mirzakhani2013growth , we prove the following result which will be used in the proof of Theorem \ref{mainthm1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-077: For any $\beta>0$, we have \be \E\left[ \left( \frac{1 {\sys(X) \right)^\beta\right] \ \asymp \ \nonumber \begin{cases 1,\ \ if\ 0<\beta<2; +\infty, \ \ if\ 2\leq \beta.. \end{cases \ene.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: For the lower bound, it follows from \cite[Theorem 4.2]{mirzakhani2013growth that \be \begin{aligned \E\left[ \left( \frac{1 {\sys(X) \right)^\beta\right] \geq \frac{\Vol_{\mathrm{WP (\sM_g^{<1 ) {V_g \asymp 1, \end{aligned \ene where $\sM_g^{<1 =\{X\in \sM_g; \ \sys(X)<1\}$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: For the upper bound, we cut $X$ along a systolic geodesic $\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-082: Then $X\setminus \gamma$ is homotopic to either $X_{g-1,2}$ or $X_{i,1}\cup X_{g-i,1}$ for $1\leq i\leq \left[\frac{g {2}\right]$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Thus, it follows from Mirzakhani's integration formula, \ie, Theorem \ref{MIF that \beqar \E\left[\left(\frac{1 {\sys(X)}\right)^\beta\right]\leq 1+\frac{1 {V_g}\int_{\M_g f(X)dX \prec 1+\frac{1 {V_g}\int_{0 ^1\left(V_{g-1,2 (t,t) +\sum_{i=1 ^{\left[\frac{g {2}\right] V_{i,1 (t)\cdot V_{g-i,1 (t) \right)\cdot t^{1-\beta dt.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: By the standard Collar Lemma (see \eg \cite{keen1974collars or \cite{buser2010geometry ), there exist at most $3g-3$ different simple closed geodesics on $X\in\M_g$ of length $\leq 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Thus, it follows from Mirzakhani's integration formula, \ie, Theorem \ref{MIF that \beqar \int_{\M_g}\left(\frac{1 {\sys(X)}\right)^\beta dX \geq \frac{1 {3g-3 \int_{\M_g f(X)dX \geq \frac{1 {3g-3 \int_0^1 t^{1-\beta V_{g-1,2 (t,t)dt.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: In this section we prove Theorem \ref{mainthm1 , assuming \eqref{e-Naud-1 of Naud.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Firstly we need the following lemma which is proved in \cite{naud2023determinants for $0<\beta<1$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: For any $0<\beta<2$, there exists a constant $C_\beta>0$ only depending on $\beta$ such that for any $g\geq 2,$ \begin{equation}\E\left[\left|\frac{\log \det (\Delta_X)}{4\pi(g-1)}\right|^\beta\right]<C_\beta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-089: We closely follow the proof of \cite[Theorem 5.1]{naud2023determinants .}
elements.sent-2411-090: First recall that the following inequality is proved in the proof of \cite[Theorem 5.1]{naud2023determinants (see Page 284 of \cite{naud2023determinants ): \begin{equation}\frac{|\log \det(\Delta_X)|}{4\pi(g-1)}\prec 1+|\log (\lambda_*(X))|+\frac{1}{\sys(X)}+\left(\frac{\log^+\sys^{-1}(X)}{\sys(X)}\frac{N_X^0(1)}{4\pi(g-1)}\right),\end{equation} where $\lambda_*(X)=\min\{\lambda_1(X),\frac{1 {4}\}$, $\log^+(x)=\max\{\log (x),0\}$ and $N_X^0(1)$ is the number of primitive closed geodesics on $X$ of length $\leq 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: For $\beta >0$, by using the elementary inequality that $(a+b)^\beta \leq 2^\beta\cdot (a^\beta+b^\beta) $ for $a,b\geq 0$, we have \be \begin{aligned \frac{|\log \det(\Delta_X)|^\beta {(4\pi(g-1))^\beta}\prec 1+|\log (\lambda_*(X))|^\beta +\left(\frac{1 {\sys(X)}\right)^\beta+\left(\frac{\log^+\sys^{-1 (X) {\sys(X)}\frac{N_X^0(1) {4\pi(g-1)}\right)^\beta.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Thus, we have \be \begin{aligned \E\left[\left(\frac{\log^+\sys^{-1 (X) {\sys(X)}\frac{N_X^0(1) {4\pi(g-1)}\right)^\beta \right] \prec \E\left[ \left(\frac{\log^+\sys^{-1 (X) {\sys(X)}\right)^\beta\right] \prec \E\left[ \left( \frac{1 {\sys(X) \right)^{\frac{\beta+2 {2 }\right] \asymp 1, \end{aligned \ene where we apply Proposition \ref{sysbetacritical in the last equation.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Then the conclusion follows from all the equations above.
elements.sent-2411-094: Recall that \begin{equation}\label{eq-def-logdetlaplace} \log \det\Delta_X=4\pi E (g-1)+\gamma_0-\int_{0}^1\frac{S_X(t)}{t}dt-\int_1^\infty\frac{S_X(t)-1}{t}dt,\end{equation} where $\gamma_0$ is the Euler constant, and \begin{equation}S_X(t)=\frac{e^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{(4\pi t)^\frac{1}{2}}\sum_{k\geq 1}\sum_{\gamma\in P} \frac{\ell(\gamma)}{2\sinh\left(\frac{k\ell(\gamma)}{2}\right)}e^{-\frac{(k\ell(\gamma))^2}{4t}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-095: Here $\mathcal{P}$ is the set of all oriented primitive closed geodesics on $X$.

elements.sent-2411-110: In this section we prove Theorem \ref{mainthm2 .}

elements.sent-2411-112: For any $ \eta\in (0, \frac{3 {16 ) $ and $\alpha \in (0,1)$, following \cite{naud2023determinants , we define \be A(g):=\left\{X\in \sM_g;\ \begin{aligned \lambda_1(X)\geq \eta \#\{(\gamma,m)\in \mathcal{P}\times \mathbb{N ,\ m\ell_\gamma(X)\leq L\}\leq C\cdot g^\alpha \end{aligned}\right\}.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-117: Then it follows from Holder's inequality, Lemma \ref{finite-C-beta and \eqref{e-l-1 that given any fixed $q\in (2,\infty),\ p\in (1,2)$ with $\frac{1 {q +\frac{1 {p =1$, for large enough $g$, \begin{equation}\label{bound-(a)} \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{V_g} \int_{A(g)^c}\left|\frac{\log Z_0^\prime(1)}{4\pi(g-1)}\right|dX \leq \left( \Prob\left(A(g)^c\right) \right)^{\frac{1}{q}} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{V_g}\int_{\M_g} \left|\frac{\log\det(\Delta_X)}{4\pi(g-1)}-E\right|^p dX\right)^{\frac{1}{p}} \leq \left( \Prob\left(A(g)^c\right) \right)^{\frac{1}{q}} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{V_g}\int_{\M_g} 2^p \cdot \left(\left|\frac{\log\det(\Delta_X)}{4\pi(g-1)}\right|^p+E^p\right) dX\right)^{\frac{1}{p}} \prec \frac{1}{g^{\frac{\eps_0}{q}}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: It follows that for $R(g)>1$, \beqar \int_{R(g) ^\infty \left|\frac{S_X(t)-1 {t}\right|dt \prec 4\pi(g-1)\int_{\eta R(g) ^\infty\frac{e^{-s {s ds \prec 4\pi(g-1)\frac{e^{-\eta R(g) {\eta R(g) .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Set \begin{equation}L_g=[4 R(g)+2]\end{equation} where $[\cdot]$ is the largest integer part.
elements.sent-2411-120: Now we separate $S_X(t)$ into three parts: \bear S_X^{L_g+ (t):=\frac{e^{-\frac{t {4 {(4\pi t)^\frac{1 {2 }\sum_{m\ell(\gamma)>L_g \frac{\ell(\gamma) {2\sinh\left(\frac{m\ell(\gamma) {2}\right) e^{-\frac{(m\ell(\gamma))^2 {4t , S_X^{s,L_g- (t):=\frac{e^{-\frac{t {4 {(4\pi t)^\frac{1 {2 }\sum_{simple \ \gamma, \ m\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g \frac{\ell(\gamma) {2\sinh\left(\frac{m\ell(\gamma) {2}\right) e^{-\frac{(m\ell(\gamma))^2 {4t , S_X^{ns,L_g- (t):=\frac{e^{-\frac{t {4 {(4\pi t)^\frac{1 {2 }\sum_{non-simple \ \gamma, \ m\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g \frac{\ell(\gamma) {2\sinh\left(\frac{m\ell(\gamma) {2}\right) e^{-\frac{(m\ell(\gamma))^2 {4t .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: First, by \eqref{deal-single-0-R(g) we have \begin{equation}\label{bound-L_g+(1)} \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\int_0^{R(g)}\frac{S_X^{L_g+}(t)}{t}dt = \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\sum_{m\ell(\gamma)\geq L_g}\int_0^{R(g)}\frac{e^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{t(4\pi t)^{\frac{1}{2}}}\frac{\ell(\gamma)}{2\sinh\frac{m\ell(\gamma)}{2}}e^{-\frac{(m\ell(\gamma))^2}{4t}}dt \prec \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\sum_{m\ell(\gamma)\geq L_g} \frac{\ell(\gamma)}{2\sinh\frac{m\ell(\gamma)}{2}}\int_0^{R(g)}\frac{e^{-\frac{(m\ell(\gamma))^2}{4t}}}{t^{\frac{3}{2}}}dt \prec \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\sum_{m\ell(\gamma)\geq L_g}\frac{1}{2\sinh \frac{m\ell(\gamma)}{2}}e^{-\frac{(m\ell(\gamma))^2}{4R(g)}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Since $X\in A(g)$, it follows from \cite[Lemma 3.2]{naud2023determinants that for all $k\geq L_g$, \begin{equation}\#\{(m,\gamma)\in N\timesP:k\leq m\ell(\gamma)\leq k+1\}\prec 4\pi(g-1)e^k.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Then we have \begin{equation}\label{bound-L_g+(2)} \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\sum_{m\ell(\gamma)\geq L_g}\frac{1}{2\sinh \frac{m\ell(\gamma)}{2}}e^{-\frac{(m\ell(\gamma))^2}{4R(g)}} \leq \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\sum_{k=L_g}^\infty \frac{1}{2\sinh \frac{k}{2}}\cdot e^{-\frac{k^2}{4R(g)}}\cdot \#\{(m,\gamma)\in \mathbb{N}\times\mathcal{P}:k\leq m\ell(\gamma)\leq k+1\} \prec \sum_{k=L_g}^\infty e^{-\frac{k^2}{4R(g)}}\cdot e^{\frac{1}{2}k} \prec \int_{L_g-1}^\infty e^{-\frac{t^2}{4R(g)}+\frac{t}{2}}dt \prec \int_{L_g-R(g)-1}^\infty e^{-\frac{s^2}{4R(g)}+\frac{R(g)}{4}} ds \prec \sqrt{R(g)} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\frac{(L_g-R(g)-1)^2}{4R(g)}} \end{aligned}\end{equation} Since $L_g>4R(g)+1$, by \eqref{bound-L_g+(1) and \eqref{bound-L_g+(2) we have \begin{equation}\label{bound-L_g+} \left| \frac{1}{V_g}\frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\int_{A(g)}\int_0^{R(g)} \frac{S_X^{L_g+}(t)}{t}dtdX \right|\prec \sqrt{R(g)} e^{-2R(g)}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-040: \begin{equation}\label{bound-L_g+(2)}  \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\sum_{m\ell(\gamma)\geq L_g}\frac{1}{2\sinh \frac{m\ell(\gamma)}{2}}e^{-\frac{(m\ell(\gamma))^2}{4R(g)}} \leq \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\sum_{k=L_g}^\infty \frac{1}{2\sinh \frac{k}{2}}\cdot e^{-\frac{k^2}{4R(g)}}\cdot \#\{(m,\gamma)\in N\timesP:k\leq m\ell(\gamma)\leq k+1\} \prec \sum_{k=L_g}^\infty e^{-\frac{k^2}{4R(g)}}\cdot e^{\frac{1}{2}k} \prec \int_{L_g-1}^\infty e^{-\frac{t^2}{4R(g)}+\frac{t}{2}}dt \prec \int_{L_g-R(g)-1}^\infty e^{-\frac{s^2}{4R(g)}+\frac{R(g)}{4}} ds \prec \sqrt{R(g)} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\frac{(L_g-R(g)-1)^2}{4R(g)}} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-123: Recall that for a simple closed geodesic $\gamma$, $X\backslash\gamma$ is homotopic to either $S_{g-1,2}$ or $X_{i,1}\cup X_{g-i,1}$ for $1\leq i\leq \left[\frac{g {2}\right]$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Then it follows from Mirzakhani's integration formula, \ie, Theorem \ref{MIF that for any $t>0$, \be \nonumber \begin{aligned \frac{1 {V_g}\int_{A(g) S_X^{s,L_g- (t)dX \leq \frac{1 {V_g}\int_{\M_g S_X^{s,L_g- (t)dX \leq \sum_{k}\frac{e^{-\frac{t {4 {(4\pi t)^{\frac12 }\int_0^\infty \frac{x {2\sinh \frac{kx {2 e^{-\frac{(kx)^2 {4t \times \frac{V_{g-1,2 (x,x)+\sum_{i=1 ^{\left[\frac{g {2}\right] V_{i,1 (x)V_{g-i,1 (x) {V_g xdx.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Thus, the above inequality together with \eqref{b-v-1 and \eqref{b-v-2 implies that \begin{equation} \frac{1}{V_g}\int_{A(g)}S_X^{s,L_g-}(t)dX \prec \sum_{k}\frac{e^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{t^{\frac12}}\int_0^\infty \frac{1}{\sinh \frac{kx}{2}}e^{-\frac{(kx)^2}{4t}}\sinh^2\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)dx \prec \sum_{k}\frac{e^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{ t^{\frac12}k}\int_0^\infty \left( e^{-\left(\frac{kx}{2\sqrt{t}}-\frac{\sqrt{t}}{2k}\right)^2+ \frac{t}{4k^2}}-e^{-\left(\frac{kx}{2\sqrt{t}}+\frac{\sqrt{t}}{2k}\right)^2+ \frac{t}{4k^2}} \right)dx = \sum_{k}\frac{e^{-\frac{t}{4}+\frac{t}{4k^2}}}{ t^{\frac12}k}\frac{2\sqrt{t}}{k}\int_{-\frac{\sqrt{t}}{2k}}^{\frac{\sqrt{t}}{2k}}e^{-s^2}ds \prec \sum_{k}\frac{e^{-\frac{t}{4}+\frac{t}{4k^2}}\sqrt{t}}{k^3} \prec \sqrt{t}. \end{equation} Therefore, it follows that \begin{equation}\label{bound simple L_g-} \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\frac{1}{V_g}\int_{A(g)} \int_0^{R(g)}\frac{S_X^{s,L_g-}(t)}{t}dt dX \prec \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\sum_k \frac{1}{k^3}\int_0^{R(g)}\frac{\sqrt{t}}{t}dt \asymp \frac{\sqrt{R(g)}}{g}. \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: For any non-simple closed primitive geodesic $\gamma$ on $X$, $\ell(\gamma)\geq 4\arcsinh 1$ (see \eg \cite{buser2010geometry ).}
elements.sent-2411-127: Then we have \begin{equation}\label{bound nonsimple SXt} \begin{aligned} S_X^{ns,L_g-}(t) \prec \frac{e^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{t^\frac{1}{2}}\sum_{m\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g}\frac{\ell(\gamma)e^{-\frac{(m\ell(\gamma))^2}{4t}}}{e^{\frac{m\ell(\gamma)}{2}}} \prec \frac{e^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{t^\frac{1}{2}}\sum_{\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g}\sum_{m\geq 1}\frac{\ell(\gamma)e^{-\frac{\ell^2(\gamma)}{4t}}}{e^{\frac{m\ell(\gamma)}{2}}} \asymp \frac{e^{-\frac{t}{4}}}{t^\frac{1}{2}}\sum_{\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g}\ell(\gamma)e^{-\frac{\ell^2(\gamma)}{4t}-\frac{\ell(\gamma)}{2}}, \end{aligned}\nonumber\end{equation} where all the $\gamma's$ are taken over all non-simple primitive closed geodesics on $X$.
elements.sent-2411-128: By \eqref{deal-single-0-R(g) we have \begin{equation}\label{bound int sxt} \begin{aligned} \int_0^{R(g)}\frac{S_X^{ns,L_g-}}{t}dt \prec \sum_{\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g}\ell(\gamma)e^{-\frac{\ell(\gamma)}{2}}\int_0^{R(g)}\frac{e^{-\frac{\ell^2(\gamma)}{4t}}}{t^{\frac{3}{2}}}dt \prec \sum_{\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g}e^{-\frac{\ell(\gamma)}{2}-\frac{\ell^2(\gamma)}{4R(g)}} = \sum_{\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g}e^{ -\frac{(\ell(\gamma)-R(g))^2}{4R(g)}+\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)} \leq \sum_{\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} As in \cite{MP19, nie2023large,wu2022random , for each non-simple closed geodesic $\gamma\subset X$, one may let $Y\subset X$ be a subsurface of geodesic boundary such that $\gamma$ is filling in $Y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Then for each non-simple closed geodesic $\gamma$ with $\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g$, by using elementary isoperimetric inequality, it is shown in \eg \cite[Proposition 7]{wu2022random that \begin{equation}\ell(\partial Y)\leq 2L_g \quad and \quad \area(Y)\leq 4L_g.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Now we count $\gamma$ according to the topology type of $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-131: Fix $\epsilon_1\in(0,1)$, for $Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}$ with $1\leq |\chi(S_{g_0,k )|\leq 16$, by Theorem \ref{wuxue counting we know that the number of filling geodesics in $Y$ with length $\leq T$ satisfies \begin{equation}\#_f(Y,T)\leq c(\epsilon_1)e^{T-\frac{1-\epsilon_1}{2}\ell(\partial Y)}\end{equation} where $c(\epsilon_1)>0$ only depends on $\epsilon_1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: Then we have \begin{equation}\label{bound 1-16} \begin{aligned} \sum_{\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}= \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}}\sum_{\gamma fills Y}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}1_{[0,L_g]}(\ell(\gamma)) \leq \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} \ \sum_{m=0}^{[L_g]} \ \sum_{m\leq \ell(\gamma)<m+1} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}1_{[0,L_g]}(\ell(\gamma)) \prec \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} \ \sum_{m=0}^{[L_g]} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-m}\cdot e^{m+1-\frac{1-\epsilon_1}{2}\ell(\partial Y)}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)) \prec L_g\cdot \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\frac{1-\epsilon_1}{2}\ell(\partial Y)}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)). \end{aligned}\end{equation} It is known from \cite[Proposition 35]{wu2022random that for any $1\leq |2g_0-2+k|\leq 16$ and $0<\eps_1<1$, we have \begin{equation}\label{bound 1-16-step2} \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{V_g}\int_{\M_g}\sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} e^{-\frac{1-\epsilon_1}{2}\ell(\partial Y)}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y))dX\prec \frac{L_g^{66}e^{\frac{L_g}{2}+\eps_1L_g} }{g}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Combine \eqref{bound int sxt , \eqref{bound 1-16 and \eqref{bound 1-16-step2 we have \begin{equation}\label{int 1-16} \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{V_g}\int_{\M_g}\sum_{1\leq |\chi(Y)|\leq 16} \sum_{\gamma fills Y} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)} 1_{[0,L_g]}(\ell(\gamma)) dX \prec \frac{L_g^{67}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}+(\frac{1}{2}+\epsilon_1)L_g}}{g}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} For $17\leq |\chi(S_{g_0,k )|\leq [\frac{4L_g {2\pi ]$, it follows from Lemma \ref{counting in buser that the number of filling closed geodesics on $Y$ with length $\leq T$ satisfies \begin{equation}\#_f(Y,T)\leq (g-1)e^{T+6}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-048: \begin{equation}\label{bound 1-16}  \sum_{\ell(\gamma)\leq L_g}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}= \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}}\sum_{\gamma fills Y}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}1_{[0,L_g]}(\ell(\gamma)) \leq \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} \ \sum_{m=0}^{[L_g]} \ \sum_{m\leq \ell(\gamma)<m+1} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}1_{[0,L_g]}(\ell(\gamma)) \prec \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} \ \sum_{m=0}^{[L_g]} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-m}\cdot e^{m+1-\frac{1-\epsilon_1}{2}\ell(\partial Y)}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)) \prec L_g\cdot \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\frac{1-\epsilon_1}{2}\ell(\partial Y)}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-133: Then \begin{equation}\label{bound 17+} \begin{aligned} \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}}\sum_{\gamma fills Y}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}1_{[0,L_g]}(\ell(\gamma)) \prec \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} \sum_{m=0}^{[L_g]}\sum_{m\leq \ell(\gamma)<m+1} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)} 1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)) \prec g \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} \sum_{m=0}^{[L_g]} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-m}\cdot e^m 1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)) \prec g\sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} L_g e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)) \prec ge^{\frac{L_g}{2}} L_g\sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\frac{\ell(\partial Y)}{4}}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)). \end{aligned}\end{equation} By \cite[Proposition 33]{wu2022random we have \begin{equation}\label{bound 17+ step2} \frac{1}{V_g} \int_{\M_g} \sum_{17\leq |\chi(Y)|\leq \left[\frac{4L_g}{2\pi}\right]}e^{-\frac{\ell(\partial Y)}{4}}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)) dX\prec\frac{L_ge^{\frac{7}{2}L_g}}{g^{17}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-052: \begin{equation}\label{bound 17+}  \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}}\sum_{\gamma fills Y}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}1_{[0,L_g]}(\ell(\gamma)) \prec \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} \sum_{m=0}^{[L_g]}\sum_{m\leq \ell(\gamma)<m+1} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)} 1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)) \prec g \sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} \sum_{m=0}^{[L_g]} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-m}\cdot e^m 1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)) \prec g\sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} L_g e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)) \prec ge^{\frac{L_g}{2}} L_g\sum_{Y\simeq S_{g_0,k}} e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\frac{\ell(\partial Y)}{4}}1_{[0,2L_g]}(\ell(\partial Y)). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-134: Combine \eqref{bound 17+ and \eqref{bound 17+ step2 we have \begin{equation}\label{int 17+} \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{V_g} \int_{\M_g} \sum_{17\leq |\chi(Y)|\leq \left[\frac{4L_g}{2\pi}\right]} \sum_{\gamma fills Y}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}-\ell(\gamma)}1_{[0,L_g]}(\ell(\gamma)) dX \prec \frac{L_g^2e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}+4L_g}}{g^{16}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Finally, combine \eqref{bound int sxt , \eqref{int 1-16 and \eqref{int 17+ we have that for any $0<\epsilon_1<1$, \begin{equation}\label{bound nonsimple L_g-} \begin{aligned} \frac{1}{V_g} \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)}\int_{A(g)} \int_0^{R(g)}\frac{S_X^{ns,L_g-}(t)}{t}dt dX \prec \frac{L_g^{67}e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}+ (\frac{1}{2}+\epsilon_1)L_g } }{g^2}+\frac{L_g^2e^{\frac{R(g)}{4}+4L_g}}{g^{17}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Since $S_X(t)=S_X^{L_g+ (t)+S_X^{s,L_g- (t)+S_X^{ns,L_g- (t)$, if we take $L_g=[4R(g)+2]$, by \eqref{bound-L_g+ , \eqref{bound simple L_g- and \eqref{bound nonsimple L_g- we have that for any $\epsilon_1>0$, \begin{equation}\label{bound-(e)} \begin{aligned} \left|\frac{1}{V_g}\int_{A(g)} \frac{1}{4\pi(g-1)} \int_0^{R(g)}\frac{S_X(t)}{t}dt dX\right| \prec \sqrt{R(g)}e^{-2R(g)}+\frac{\sqrt{R(g)}}{g}+\frac{R(g)^{67}e^{(\frac{9}{4}+\epsilon_1)R(g)}}{g^2}+\frac{R(g)^2e^{\frac{65}{4}R(g)}}{g^{17}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{rem* Actually if we take $L_g=[kR(g)+2]$ for $k>2$, the argument above also works.. \end{rem*.}}}}}}}}
